summary: Renaissance-era Florentine political theorist and author of The Prince and Discourses on Livy. Known for his concepts of virtu and fortuna, his claim that it is 'better to be feared than loved,' his condemnation of mercenaries, and his status as the 'first modern philosopher' according to Leo Strauss.

works.name: The Prince
works.description: Machiavelli's most famous work, a political treatise in the 'mirror for princes' genre, dedicated to Lorenzo de Medici. It argues that a ruler must embody virtu, be feared rather than loved, tame Fortuna (compared to a raging river and a woman that must be 'mastered'), and avoid mercenaries. Cesare Borgia is held up as a model ruler. The work identifies Theseus, Moses, Romulus, and Cyrus as 'outstanding men' who attained power through virtu. Frederick the Great published a critique of it edited by Voltaire.

clues.clue: Better to be feared than loved; ruler must embody virtu
examples: the claim that it is better to be feared than loved appears in what author's treatise The Prince?
Leaders require virtu and should aspire to be feared rather than loved

clues.clue: Fortuna compared to a raging river and a woman that must be 'beaten and mauled' / 'mastered'
examples: this man declared that 'it is necessary, in order to keep [Fortuna] under, to beat and maul her.'
Machiavelli claims that this concept is a lady that must be beaten

clues.clue: Condemns mercenaries; says they have no loyalty to God or a leader
examples: argues that mercenaries shouldn't be used, since they have no loyalty to either God or a leader
a treatise that disparages mercenaries while praising Cesare Borgia

clues.clue: Cesare Borgia held up as a model ruler
examples: he identified Theseus, Moses, Romulus, and Cyrus as four 'outstanding men' who attained power through virtu in a work that holds up Cesare Borgia as a model ruler

clues.clue: Dedicated to Lorenzo de Medici; initially rejected because dedicatee was distracted by a gift of dogs
examples: fortune compared to a raging river and to a woman that must be 'mastered' in a treatise dedicated to Lorenzo de Medici
this thinker's best-known book was initially rejected by its dedicatee because the dedicatee was too distracted by a gift of a pair of dogs

clues.clue: Frederick the Great published a critique (Anti-Machiavel) edited by Voltaire
examples: Frederick II of Prussia wrote a tract against this man
Shortly after being elected king, Frederick the Great published a critique of this man edited by Voltaire.

clues.clue: Ruler must be like a fox (cunning) and a lion (strength)
examples: the title figure must 'protect himself from traps' and 'defend himself from wolves' like the fox and lion
advises to be like a 'lion' and a 'fox'

clues.clue: Uses metaphor of damming a river and beating a woman to describe mastering Fortune; poetic ending quoting 'ancient valor is not yet dead'
examples: This man used a poetic quote beginning 'Virtue shall take up arms against savagery' to inspire his countrymen, at the end a book that uses the metaphors of a damming a river and beating a woman to describe mastering Fortune.

clues.clue: Contrasted armed vs. unarmed prophets (armed prophets succeed); used Savonarola as example of unarmed prophet
examples: He contrasted the success of prophets who are armed and those who are not
he uses a contemporary of his who also influenced Pico della Mirandola

clues.clue: 1958 analysis calls it 'both a treatise and a tract for the times' with 'a traditional exterior and a revolutionary center'
examples: A 1958 analysis of this author's major work calls it 'both a treatise and a tract for the times' with 'a traditional exterior and a revolutionary center.'

works.name: Discourses on Livy
works.description: Machiavelli's commentary on the Roman historian Livy's Ab Urbe Condita. It espouses the virtues of Roman republicanism and discusses the 'origin of cities,' contrasting Athens with Sparta. He cited Rome's ability to switch between strategies of Fabius and Scipio as evidence of republics' superiority to kings. The work aims to 'open a new route' by basing judgments on 'respect for antiquity.'

clues.clue: Commentary on Ab Urbe Condita (Livy's history of Rome); espouses Roman republicanism
examples: This man's study of the Roman text Ab urbe condita informed his views on republics.
this man contrasts Athens with Sparta, where a 'wise legislator' made laws that lasted 800 years, in a book on the Roman commonwealth as seen in the 'first decade' of Ab Urbe Condita

clues.clue: Opening resolves to 'open a new route' by basing judgments on 'respect for antiquity'
examples: The opening of a book by this philosopher resolves to 'open a new route' by basing judgments on 'respect for antiquity'

clues.clue: Rome's ability to switch between Fabius and Scipio shows superiority of republics to kings
examples: This thinker cited Rome's ability to switch between the strategies of Fabius and Scipio during the Second Punic War as an example of the superiority of republics to kings.

clues.clue: Called into question how much Machiavelli believed in realism of The Prince, since Discourses espouses republicanism
examples: how much he actually believed in realism is called into question due to this work. In this work, Machiavelli espouses the virtues of Roman republicanism

works.name: Leo Strauss and Reception
works.description: Leo Strauss considered Machiavelli the 'first modern philosopher' and the origin of the ancients-moderns divide. His Thoughts on Machiavelli analyzes The Prince and Discourses on Livy, arguing that Machiavelli's 'anti-theological ire' was the source of modernity. Harvey Mansfield argued Machiavelli sought to overthrow Christianity. Strauss's students (Allan Bloom, Harry Jaffa) split into East Coast and West Coast schools. James Burnham's The Machiavellians shows how his tradition persists through Robert Michels.

clues.clue: Leo Strauss called him the 'first modern philosopher' / 'first of the moderns'
examples: He is called the 'first of the moderns' in lectures delivered at the University of Chicago collected as the 'thoughts' on him by Leo Strauss.
Leo Strauss claimed that this man was the first 'modern' thinker

clues.clue: Strauss's Thoughts on Machiavelli analyzes both The Prince and Discourses; focused on anticlericalism
examples: Leo Strauss's Thoughts on this thinker were discussed in a work by Harvey Mansfield

This philosopher pinpointed Machiavelli as the first modern thinker in his ancients-modern dichotomy. This philosopher analyzed the relationship between The Prince and Discourses on Livy in his Thoughts on Machiavelli.

clues.clue: Harvey Mansfield argued Machiavelli sought to overthrow Christianity; focus on individual virtu as key to 'manliness'
examples: Harvey Mansfield, who argued that this man sought to overthrow Christianity
Harvey Mansfield believed that this man's focus on individual virtu was a key development in the history of 'manliness.'

clues.clue: James Burnham's The Machiavellians shows his tradition persists through Robert Michels's 'limits of democracy'
examples: This man titles a book showing how his tradition persists through Robert Michels' analysis of the 'limits of democracy,' written by James Burnham.

clues.clue: Foundational influence on the realism paradigm in international relations
examples: Foundational influences on realism include this Italian author, who explained that it was better to be feared than loved in The Prince.

works.name: The Mandrake (La Mandragola)
works.description: Machiavelli's comedy play in which the marriage-broker Ligurio helps Callimaco trick the infertile Nicia into thinking his wife Lucrezia must take a mandrake fertility potion that will kill the first man who sleeps with her. Callimaco disguises himself as a doctor, sleeps with Lucrezia, and she becomes his lover. Athol Fugard's Serpent Players adapted it as The Cure.

clues.clue: Callimaco tricks Nicia; Lucrezia takes mandrake potion; Callimaco sleeps with her and she becomes his lover
examples: Callimaco's trickery allows him to sleep with Lucrezia in that play by him, named for the plant used to make the potion.
Callimaco disguising himself as a doctor to dupe the infertile Nicia

clues.clue: Marriage-broker Ligurio is involved in the scheme
examples: the marriage-broker Ligurio is involved in a scheme surrounding a potion

clues.clue: Athol Fugard's Serpent Players adapted it as The Cure
examples: The first production of Athol Fugard's Serpent Players was an adaptation of this play entitled The Cure.
Fugard led a theater group called the Serpent Players in a 1963 production of The Cure, an adaptation of this author's play The Mandrake.

works.name: Machiavel in English Renaissance Drama
works.description: Machiavelli became a stock character ('Machiavel') in English Renaissance drama, synonymous with ruthless scheming. The ghost of 'Machiavel' delivers the opening monologue of Christopher Marlowe's The Jew of Malta, declaring 'religion but a childish toy.' In Shakespeare's Henry VI Part 3, Richard III boasts he can set 'Machiavel to school.'

clues.clue: Ghost of 'Machiavel' opens Marlowe's The Jew of Malta: 'religion but a childish toy' and 'Albeit the world thinks Machiavel is dead'
examples: A fictional version of this man boasts, 'Admired I am of those that hate me most' in Christopher Marlowe's Jew of Malta.
this person tells those who think him dead that his soul has 'flown beyond the Alps' and professes to 'count religion but a childish toy'

clues.clue: In Henry VI Part 3, Richard III claims he can set 'the murderous Machiavel to school'
examples: A character in Henry VI Part 3 boasts 'I can add colours to the chameleon' and set this 'murderous' person 'to school.'

clues.clue: In Henry VI Part I, York calls Alencon a 'notorious Machiavel' when Joan of Arc reveals Alencon as her lover
examples: In Henry VI, Part I, York calls Alencon a 'notorious Machiavel'

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Florentine political theorist and official. Imprisoned after the Sack of Prato; complained of farm life in letters to Francesco Vettori. J.G.A. Pocock links him with Francesco Guicciardini in The Machiavellian Moment. Innocent Gentillet published a denunciation after the St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre. Also wrote Clizia (based on a lost Plautus play), adapted Terence's Andria, wrote the novella 'Belfagor the Archdemon,' and wrote intermedio songs. De Waal applied his ideas to chimpanzee power politics.

comprehensive_summary:
Niccolo Machiavelli is a Renaissance-era Florentine political theorist, most famous for The Prince and the Discourses on Livy. He is one of the most frequently clued philosophers in quizbowl, appearing across philosophy, history, and literature categories.

The Prince is a political treatise in the 'mirror for princes' genre, dedicated to Lorenzo de Medici (whose initial rejection was supposedly because he was distracted by a gift of dogs). It argues that a ruler must embody virtu, be feared rather than loved, tame Fortuna (compared to a raging river and a woman that must be 'mastered'), avoid mercenaries, and be like both a fox (cunning) and a lion (strength). Cesare Borgia is held up as a model ruler, and Theseus, Moses, Romulus, and Cyrus are identified as 'outstanding men.' Armed prophets succeed while unarmed ones (like Savonarola) fail. Frederick the Great published a critique (Anti-Machiavel) edited by Voltaire.

The Discourses on Livy is a commentary on Livy's Ab Urbe Condita that espouses Roman republicanism. It discusses the 'origin of cities,' contrasts Athens with Sparta, and cites Rome's ability to switch between Fabius and Scipio as evidence of republics' superiority. The work aims to 'open a new route' based on 'respect for antiquity.'

Leo Strauss considered Machiavelli the 'first modern philosopher' and analyzed him in Thoughts on Machiavelli, arguing his 'anti-theological ire' was the source of modernity. Harvey Mansfield argued he sought to overthrow Christianity. James Burnham's The Machiavellians traces his influence through Robert Michels.

Machiavelli wrote The Mandrake (La Mandragola), a comedy in which Callimaco tricks the infertile Nicia into letting him sleep with Lucrezia through a fake mandrake potion scheme. Athol Fugard's Serpent Players adapted it as The Cure. In English Renaissance drama, 'Machiavel' became a stock villain: the ghost of Machiavel opens Marlowe's The Jew of Malta declaring 'religion but a childish toy,' and Richard III in Henry VI Part 3 boasts he can set 'the murderous Machiavel to school.'

Additional biographical facts: Machiavelli was imprisoned after the Sack of Prato, complained of farm life to Francesco Vettori, and J.G.A. Pocock links him with Guicciardini in The Machiavellian Moment. He adapted Terence's Andria, wrote Clizia (based on Plautus), the novella 'Belfagor the Archdemon,' and intermedio songs. Frans de Waal applied his ideas to chimpanzee power politics.
recursive_suggestions: The Prince - frequently appears as its own answerline
The Mandrake (La Mandragola) - occasionally its own answerline
